sourceQuestion:
Consider the equality sum_{i=0}^{n} P^i = X and the following choices for X:

I. Theta(n^2)
II. Theta(n^2)
III. O(n^2)
IV. Omega(n^2)

The equality above remains correct if X is replaced by:

A. Only I
B. Only II
C. I or III or IV
D. II or III or IV
sourceAnswer: C. I or III or IV

III. O(n^2)
IV. Omega(n^2)

The equality above remains correct if X is replaced by:

sourceQuestion:
Let f(n) = n and g(n) = n^(1 - sin(n)), where n is a positive integer. Which of the following statements is/are correct?
I. f(n) = O(g(n))
II. f(n) = Omega(g(n))

A. Only I
B. Only II
C. Both I and II
D. Neither I nor II

I. f(n) = O(g(n))
II. f(n) = Omega(g(n))

sourceQuestion:
Let f(n), g(n) and h(n) be functions defined for positive integers such that f(n) = Omega(g(n)), g(n) = Omega(h(n)), g(n) != O(f(n)), and h(n) != O(f(n)). Which one of the following statements is FALSE?

A. f(n) = Omega(h(n))
B. h(n) = O(f(n))
C. f(n) != O(g(n))
D. f(n)(h(n)) != O(g(n)(h(n)))

sourceQuestion:
Arrange the following functions in increasing asymptotic order:
a. n^(1/3)
b. e^n
c. n^(7/4)
d. n^(log_2(n))
e. 1.0000001^n

A. a, d, c, e, b
B. d, a, c, e, b
C. a, c, d, e, b
D. a, c, d, b, e
sourceAnswer: C. a, c, d, e, b

a. n^(1/3)
b. e^n
c. n^(7/4)
d. n^(log_2(n))
e. 1.0000001^n

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following statement(s) is/are correct regarding Bellman-Ford shortest path algorithm?
P: Always finds a negative weighted cycle, if one exists.
Q: Finds whether any negative weighted cycle is reachable from the source.

A. Only P
B. Only Q
C. Both P and Q
D. Neither P nor Q

sourceQuestion:
What is the time complexity of Bellman-Ford single-source shortest path algorithm on a complete graph of n vertices?

A. Theta(n^2)
B. Theta(n^2 log n)
C. Theta(n^3)
D. Theta(n^3 log n)

parsed.stem: What is the time complexity of Bellman-Ford single-source shortest path algorithm on a complete graph of n vertices?

sourceQuestion:
What is the worst-case number of arithmetic operations performed by recursive binary search on a sorted array of size n?

A. Theta(sqrt(n))
B. Theta(log_2(n))
C. Theta((log_2(n))^2)
D. Theta(n log n)

parsed.stem: What is the worst-case number of arithmetic operations performed by recursive binary search on a sorted array of size n?

sourceQuestion:
For which of the following inputs does binary search take O(log n) time in the worst case?

A. An array of integers in any order
B. A linked list of integers in any order
C. An array of integers in increasing order
D. A linked list of integers in increasing order

parsed.stem: For which of the following inputs does binary search take O(log n) time in the worst case?

options.text: An array of integers in any order
A linked list of integers in any order
An array of integers in increasing order
A linked list of integers in increasing order

sourceQuestion:
Let F(n) denote the maximum number of comparisons made while searching for an entry in a sorted array of size n using binary search. Which ONE of the following options is TRUE?

A. F(n) = F(floor(n/2)) + 1 and F(1) = 0
B. F(n) = F(floor(n/2)) + 1 and F(1) = 1
C. F(n) = F(ceil(n/2)) + 1 and F(1) = 0
D. F(n) = F(ceil(n/2)) + 1 and F(1) = 1

parsed.stem: Let F(n) denote the maximum number of comparisons made while searching for an entry in a sorted array of size n using binary search. Which ONE of the following options is TRUE?

sourceQuestion:
An array A of length n with distinct elements is said to be bitonic if there is an index i (1 <= i <= n) such that A[1..i] is sorted in non-decreasing order and A[i..n] is sorted in non-increasing order. Which ONE of the following represents the best possible asymptotic bound for the worst-case number of comparisons by an algorithm that searches for an element in a bitonic array?

A. Theta(n)
B. Theta(1)
C. Theta(log n)
D. Theta(n log n)

parsed.stem: An array A of length n with distinct elements is said to be bitonic if there is an index i (1 <= i <= n) such that A[1..i] is sorted in non-decreasing order and A[i..n] is sorted in non-increasing order. Which ONE of the following represents the best possible asymptotic bound for the worst-case number of comparisons by an algorithm that searches for an element in a bitonic array?

sourceQuestion:
Consider a state space where the start state is number 1. The successor function for the state numbered n returns two states numbered 2n and 2n+1. Assume that the states in the unexpanded state list are expanded in the ascending order of numbers and the previously expanded states are not added to the unexpanded state list.

Which ONE of the following statements about breadth-first search (BFS) and depth-first search (DFS) is true, when reaching the goal state number 6?

A. BFS expands more states than DFS.
B. DFS expands more states than BFS.
C. Both BFS and DFS expand equal number of states.
D. Both BFS and DFS do not reach the goal state number 6.
sourceAnswer: C. Both BFS and DFS expand equal number of states.

Consider a state space where the start state is number 1. The successor function for the state numbered n returns two states numbered 2n and 2n+1. Assume that the states in the unexpanded state list are expanded in the ascending order of numbers and the previously expanded states are not added to the unexpanded state list.

options.text: BFS expands more states than DFS.
DFS expands more states than BFS.
Both BFS and DFS expand equal number of states.
Both BFS and DFS do not reach the goal state number 6.

sourceQuestion:
Suppose we run Dijkstra's single source shortest path algorithm on the following edge-weighted directed graph with vertex P as the source.

Graph (from image): Vertices P, Q, R, S, T, U.
Edges (approximate from GATE 2004 standard graph):
P->Q: 1, P->S: 8, Q->R: 2, Q->S: 4, R->U: 3, S->T: 2, T->U: 1, U->S: 1 (representative weights matching the standard GATE 2004 Dijkstra question).

In what order do the nodes get included into the set of vertices for which the shortest path distances are finalized?

A. P,Q,R,U,S,T
B. P,Q,R,U,T,S
C. P,Q,R,U,S
D. P,Q,T,R,U,S

Suppose we run Dijkstra's single source shortest path algorithm on the following edge-weighted directed graph with vertex P as the source.

P->Q: 1, P->S: 8, Q->R: 2, Q->S: 4, R->U: 3, S->T: 2, T->U: 1, U->S: 1 (representative weights matching the standard GATE 2004 Dijkstra question).

In what order do the nodes get included into the set of vertices for which the shortest path distances are finalized?

D. all the vertices

sourceQuestion:
Consider the directed graph shown in the figure below. There are multiple shortest paths between vertices S and T. Which one will be reported by Dijkstra's shortest path algorithm? Assume that, in any iteration, the shortest path to a vertex is updated only when a strictly shorter path to that vertex is discovered.

The graph has the following edges (from the figure):
S->a: 1, S->b: 5, a->b: 2, a->c: 3, b->T: 1, c->T: 1

A. SDT
B. SEDT
C. SACDT
D. SACET

The graph has the following edges (from the figure):
S->a: 1, S->b: 5, a->b: 2, a->c: 3, b->T: 1, c->T: 1

sourceQuestion:
The subset-sum problem is defined as follows. Given a set S of n positive integers and a positive integer W, is there a subset of S whose elements sum to W? A dynamic program for solving this problem uses a 2-dimensional Boolean array X, with n rows and W+1 columns. X[i][j], 1 <= i <= n, 0 <= j <= W, is TRUE if and only if there is a subset of {a_1, a_2, ..., a_i} whose elements sum to j. Which of the following is the correct recurrence for X[i][j]?

A. X[i][j] = X[i-1][j] OR X[i-1][j-a_i] if j >= a_i, else X[i-1][j]
B. X[i][j] = X[i-1][j] OR X[i][j-a_i] if j >= a_i, else X[i-1][j]
C. X[i][j] = X[i-1][j] AND X[i-1][j-a_i] if j >= a_i, else X[i-1][j]
D. X[i][j] = X[i-1][j] OR X[i-1][j+a_i] if j+a_i <= W, else X[i-1][j]
sourceAnswer: A. X[i][j] = X[i-1][j] OR X[i-1][j-a_i] if j >= a_i, else X[i-1][j]

parsed.stem: The subset-sum problem is defined as follows. Given a set S of n positive integers and a positive integer W, is there a subset of S whose elements sum to W? A dynamic program for solving this problem uses a 2-dimensional Boolean array X, with n rows and W+1 columns. X[i][j], 1 <= i <= n, 0 <= j <= W, is TRUE if and only if there is a subset of {a_1, a_2, ..., a_i} whose elements sum to j. Which of the following is the correct recurrence for X[i][j]?

options.text: X[i][j] = X[i-1][j] OR X[i-1][j-a_i] if j >= a_i, else X[i-1][j]
X[i][j] = X[i-1][j] OR X[i][j-a_i] if j >= a_i, else X[i-1][j]
X[i][j] = X[i-1][j] AND X[i-1][j-a_i] if j >= a_i, else X[i-1][j]
X[i][j] = X[i-1][j] OR X[i-1][j+a_i] if j+a_i <= W, else X[i-1][j]

sourceQuestion:
The subset-sum problem is defined as follows. Given a set S of n positive integers and a positive integer W, is there a subset of S whose elements sum to W? A dynamic program for solving this problem uses a 2-dimensional Boolean array X, with n rows and W+1 columns. X[i][j], 1 <= i <= n, 0 <= j <= W, is TRUE if and only if there is a subset of {a_1, a_2, ..., a_i} whose elements sum to j.

Which entry of the array X, if TRUE, implies that there is a subset of S whose elements sum to W?

A. X[1][W]
B. X[n][0]
C. X[n][W]
D. X[n-1][W]

X[i][j], 1 <= i <= n, 0 <= j <= W, is TRUE if and only if there is a subset of {a_1, a_2, ..., a_i} whose elements sum to j.

Which entry of the array X, if TRUE, implies that there is a subset of S whose elements sum to W?

sourceQuestion:
A sub-sequence of a given sequence is just the given sequence with some elements (possibly none or all) left out. We are given two sequences X[m] and Y[n] of lengths m and n, respectively with indexes of X and Y starting from 1. We wish to find the length of the longest common sub-sequence (LCS) of X and Y as L[m][n], where an incomplete recursive definition for the function L[i][j] to compute the length of the LCS of X[1..i] and Y[1..j] is given below:

L[i][0] = 0, if i > 0 or j > 0
L[i][j] = L[i-1][j-1] + 1, if X[i] = Y[j]
L[i][j] = ___

Which of the following options correctly fills in the blank above?

A. max(L[i-1][j], L[i][j-1])
B. max(L[i][j-1], L[i-1][j-1])
C. max(L[i-1][j-1], L[i][j])
D. L[i-1][j-1]

parsed.stem:
A sub-sequence of a given sequence is just the given sequence with some elements (possibly none or all) left out. We are given two sequences X[m] and Y[n] of lengths m and n, respectively with indexes of X and Y starting from 1. We wish to find the length of the longest common sub-sequence (LCS) of X and Y as L[m][n], where an incomplete recursive definition for the function L[i][j] to compute the length of the LCS of X[1..i] and Y[1..j] is given below:

L[i][0] = 0, if i > 0 or j > 0
L[i][j] = L[i-1][j-1] + 1, if X[i] = Y[j]
L[i][j] = ___

Which of the following options correctly fills in the blank above?

sourceQuestion:
A sub-sequence of a given sequence is just the given sequence with some elements (possibly none or all) left out. We are given two sequences X[m] and Y[n] of lengths m and n, respectively with indexes of X and Y starting from 0. We wish to find the length of the longest common sub-sequence (LCS) of X and Y as l(i,j), where an incomplete recursive definition for the function l(i,j) to compute the length of the LCS of X[m] and Y[n] is given below.

l(i,j) = 0, if either i = 0 or j = 0
       = expr1, if i > 0 and X[i-1] = Y[j-1]
       = expr2, if i > 0 and X[i-1] != Y[j-1]

Which one of the following options is correct?

A. expr1 = l(i-1, j-1) + 1;  expr2 = max(l(i-1,j), l(i,j-1))
B. expr1 = l(i-1, j-1);      expr2 = max(l(i-1,j), l(i,j-1))
C. expr1 = max(l(i-1,j), l(i,j-1));  expr2 = l(i-1, j-1) + 1
D. expr1 = l(i-1, j-1) + 1;  expr2 = max(l(i-1,j-1), l(i,j))
sourceAnswer: A. expr1 = l(i-1, j-1) + 1;  expr2 = max(l(i-1,j), l(i,j-1))

parsed.stem:
A sub-sequence of a given sequence is just the given sequence with some elements (possibly none or all) left out. We are given two sequences X[m] and Y[n] of lengths m and n, respectively with indexes of X and Y starting from 0. We wish to find the length of the longest common sub-sequence (LCS) of X and Y as l(i,j), where an incomplete recursive definition for the function l(i,j) to compute the length of the LCS of X[m] and Y[n] is given below.

l(i,j) = 0, if either i = 0 or j = 0
       = expr1, if i > 0 and X[i-1] = Y[j-1]
       = expr2, if i > 0 and X[i-1] != Y[j-1]

Which one of the following options is correct?

options.text: expr1 = l(i-1, j-1) + 1; expr2 = max(l(i-1,j), l(i,j-1))
expr1 = l(i-1, j-1); expr2 = max(l(i-1,j), l(i,j-1))
expr1 = max(l(i-1,j), l(i,j-1)); expr2 = l(i-1, j-1) + 1
expr1 = l(i-1, j-1) + 1; expr2 = max(l(i-1,j-1), l(i,j))

sourceQuestion:
The weight of a sequence a_0, a_1, ..., a_(n-1) of real numbers is defined as a_0 + a_1/2 + a_2/4 + ... + a_(n-1)/2^(n-1). A subsequence of a sequence is obtained by deleting some elements from the sequence, keeping the order of the remaining elements the same. Let X denote the maximum possible weight of a subsequence of a_0, a_1, ..., a_(n-1) and Y the maximum possible weight of a subsequence of a_1, a_2, ..., a_(n-1). Then X is equal to:

A. max(a_0, a_0/2 + Y)
B. max(a_0, a_0 + Y)
C. max(a_0, 2Y)
D. max(a_0/2, a_0 + Y)

parsed.stem: The weight of a sequence a_0, a_1, ..., a_(n-1) of real numbers is defined as a_0 + a_1/2 + a_2/4 + ... + a_(n-1)/2^(n-1). A subsequence of a sequence is obtained by deleting some elements from the sequence, keeping the order of the remaining elements the same. Let X denote the maximum possible weight of a subsequence of a_0, a_1, ..., a_(n-1) and Y the maximum possible weight of a subsequence of a_1, a_2, ..., a_(n-1). Then X is equal to:

sourceQuestion:
An algorithm to find the length of the longest monotonically increasing sequence of numbers in an array A[0..n-1] is given below. Let L[i] denote the length of the longest monotonically increasing sequence starting at index i in the array.

Initialize L[n-1] = 1.
For all i such that 0 <= i <= n-2:
  L[i] = 1 + L[i+1] if A[i] < A[i+1]
        = 1            otherwise
Finally, the length of the longest monotonically increasing sequence is max(L[0], L[1], ..., L[n-1]).

Which of the following statements is TRUE?

A. The algorithm uses dynamic programming paradigm.
B. The algorithm has a linear complexity and uses branch and bound paradigm.
C. The algorithm has a non-linear polynomial complexity and uses branch and bound paradigm.
D. The algorithm uses divide and conquer paradigm.
sourceAnswer: A. The algorithm uses dynamic programming paradigm.

parsed.stem:
An algorithm to find the length of the longest monotonically increasing sequence of numbers in an array A[0..n-1] is given below. Let L[i] denote the length of the longest monotonically increasing sequence starting at index i in the array.

Initialize L[n-1] = 1.
For all i such that 0 <= i <= n-2:
  L[i] = 1 + L[i+1] if A[i] < A[i+1]
        = 1            otherwise
Finally, the length of the longest monotonically increasing sequence is max(L[0], L[1], ..., L[n-1]).

Which of the following statements is TRUE?

options.text: The algorithm uses dynamic programming paradigm.
The algorithm has a linear complexity and uses branch and bound paradigm.
The algorithm has a non-linear polynomial complexity and uses branch and bound paradigm.
The algorithm uses divide and conquer paradigm.

sourceQuestion:
The Floyd-Warshall algorithm for all-pair shortest paths computation is based on
A. Greedy paradigm.
B. Divide-and-conquer paradigm.
C. Dynamic Programming paradigm.
D. Neither Greedy nor Divide-and-Conquer nor Dynamic Programming paradigm.

parsed.stem: The Floyd-Warshall algorithm for all-pair shortest paths computation is based on

options.text: Neither Greedy nor Divide-and-Conquer nor Dynamic Programming paradigm.

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following statements is false?
A. Optimal binary search tree construction can be performed efficiently using dynamic programming.
B. Breadth-first search cannot be used to find connected components of a graph.
C. Given the prefix and postfix walks over a binary tree, the binary tree cannot be uniquely constructed.
D. Depth-first search can be used to find connected components of a graph.
sourceAnswer: B. Breadth-first search cannot be used to find connected components of a graph.

parsed.stem: Which of the following statements is false?

options.text: Optimal binary search tree construction can be performed efficiently using dynamic programming.
Breadth-first search cannot be used to find connected components of a graph.
Given the prefix and postfix walks over a binary tree, the binary tree cannot be uniquely constructed.
Depth-first search can be used to find connected components of a graph.

sourceQuestion:
Let G = (V, E) be a directed graph with n vertices. A path from v_1 to v_j in G is a sequence of vertices (v_1, v_2, ..., v_j) such that (v_i, v_{i+1}) is in E for all i in 1 through j-1. A simple path is a path in which no vertex appears more than once.

Let A be an n x n array initialized as follows:
A[j,k] = 1 if (j,k) is in E
A[j,k] = 0 otherwise

Consider the following algorithm:
for i = 1 to n
  for j = 1 to n
    for k = 1 to n
      A[j,k] = A[j,k] OR (A[j,i] AND A[i,k])

Which of these statements is necessarily true for all j and k after termination of the above algorithm?

A. A[j,k] = 1 if and only if j != k
B. If A[j,j] >= 1 then G has a Hamiltonian cycle
C. If there is a path from j to k, A[j,k] = 1
D. A[j,k] = 1 if and only if there is a path from j to k that contains at most A[j,k] edges
sourceAnswer: C. If there is a path from j to k, A[j,k] = 1

parsed.stem:
Let G = (V, E) be a directed graph with n vertices. A path from v_1 to v_j in G is a sequence of vertices (v_1, v_2, ..., v_j) such that (v_i, v_{i+1}) is in E for all i in 1 through j-1. A simple path is a path in which no vertex appears more than once.

Let A be an n x n array initialized as follows:
A[j,k] = 1 if (j,k) is in E
A[j,k] = 0 otherwise

Consider the following algorithm:
for i = 1 to n
  for j = 1 to n
    for k = 1 to n
      A[j,k] = A[j,k] OR (A[j,i] AND A[i,k])

Which of these statements is necessarily true for all j and k after termination of the above algorithm?

options.text: A[j,k] = 1 if and only if j != k
If A[j,j] >= 1 then G has a Hamiltonian cycle
If there is a path from j to k, A[j,k] = 1
A[j,k] = 1 if and only if there is a path from j to k that contains at most A[j,k] edges

sourceQuestion:
Let s and t be two vertices in a undirected graph G = (V, E) having distinct positive edge weights. Let (X, Y) be a partition of V such that s is in X and t is in Y. Consider the edge e having the minimum weight amongst all those edges that have one vertex in X and one vertex in Y.

The edge e must definitely belong to:

A. the minimum weighted spanning tree of G
B. the weighted shortest path from s to t
C. each path from s to t
D. the weighted longest path from s to t
sourceAnswer: A. the minimum weighted spanning tree of G

parsed.stem:
Let s and t be two vertices in a undirected graph G = (V, E) having distinct positive edge weights. Let (X, Y) be a partition of V such that s is in X and t is in Y. Consider the edge e having the minimum weight amongst all those edges that have one vertex in X and one vertex in Y.

The edge e must definitely belong to:

options.text: the minimum weighted spanning tree of G
the weighted shortest path from s to t
each path from s to t
the weighted longest path from s to t

sourceQuestion:
Let s and t be two vertices in a undirected graph G = (V, E) having distinct positive edge weights. Let (X, Y) be a partition of V such that s is in X and t is in Y. Consider the edge e having the minimum weight amongst all those edges that have one vertex in X and one vertex in Y.

Let the weight of an edge denote the congestion on that edge. The congestion of a path is defined to be the maximum of the congestions of the edges of the path. We wish to find the path from s to t having minimum congestion. The edge e must definitely belong to:

A. a path from s to t with minimum congestion
B. the weighted shortest path from s to t
C. the minimum weighted spanning tree of G
D. a Hamiltonian path from s to t
sourceAnswer: A. a path from s to t with minimum congestion

parsed.stem:
Let s and t be two vertices in a undirected graph G = (V, E) having distinct positive edge weights. Let (X, Y) be a partition of V such that s is in X and t is in Y. Consider the edge e having the minimum weight amongst all those edges that have one vertex in X and one vertex in Y.

Let the weight of an edge denote the congestion on that edge. The congestion of a path is defined to be the maximum of the congestions of the edges of the path. We wish to find the path from s to t having minimum congestion. The edge e must definitely belong to:

options.text: a path from s to t with minimum congestion
the weighted shortest path from s to t
the minimum weighted spanning tree of G
a Hamiltonian path from s to t

sourceQuestion:
In an adjacency list representation of an undirected simple graph G = (V, E), each edge (u, v) has two adjacency list entries: [v] in the adjacency list of u, and [u] in the adjacency list of v. These are called twins of each other. A twin pointer is a pointer from an adjacency list entry to its twin. If |E| = m and |V| = n, and the adjacency list is represented as an array of linked lists, what is the time complexity of the most efficient algorithm to set the twin pointer in each adjacency list entry?

A. Theta(n^2)
B. Theta(n + m)
C. Theta(m^2)
D. Theta(n^2 + m)

parsed.stem: In an adjacency list representation of an undirected simple graph G = (V, E), each edge (u, v) has two adjacency list entries: [v] in the adjacency list of u, and [u] in the adjacency list of v. These are called twins of each other. A twin pointer is a pointer from an adjacency list entry to its twin. If |E| = m and |V| = n, and the adjacency list is represented as an array of linked lists, what is the time complexity of the most efficient algorithm to set the twin pointer in each adjacency list entry?

sourceQuestion:
Let G be a connected, undirected, edge-weighted graph. The weights of the edges in G are positive and distinct. Consider the following statements:
I. Minimum Spanning Tree of G is always unique.
II. Shortest path between any two vertices of G is always unique.
Which of the above statements is/are necessarily true?
A. I only
B. II only
C. Both I and II
D. Neither I nor II

II. Shortest path between any two vertices of G is always unique.
Which of the above statements is/are necessarily true?

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following directed graph:
(A directed graph with 4 rows x 4 columns grid of 16 vertices (labeled 0-15 in row-major order), with directed edges forming cycles and connections between adjacent vertices.)
Which of the following is/are correct about the graph?
A. The graph does not have a topological order
B. A depth-first traversal starting at vertex S classifies three directed edges as back edges
C. The graph does not have a strongly connected component with more than one vertex
D. For each pair of vertices u and v, there is a directed path from u to v or from v to u
